sections.heading: Overview
sections.body:
This page explains how to configure advanced authentication and authorization settings for
database users in Atlas Data Federation. This includes available authentication methods and how to
manage users using the Atlas CLI, the Atlas Administration API, and the Atlas UI.

> **Note:** 
>
> Atlas supports a maximum of 100 database users per Atlas
> project by default. If you require more than 100 database users on a
> project, you can use the [Atlas Administration API](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-setgrouplimit) to increase the limit. For assistance, contact
> [Atlas support](https://cloud.mongodb.com/support).

sections.heading: Required Access
sections.body:
To use the advanced database user configurations, you must have [Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>), [Project Owner](<Project Owner>), or [Project Database Access Admin](<Project Database Access Admin>) access in
Atlas.

sections.heading: Authenticate Database Users
sections.body:
Atlas (+adf+) offers the following forms of authentication for database users:

Password

X.509 Certificates

AWS IAM

sections.heading: Authenticate Database Users
sections.body:
[SCRAM](manual/core/security-scram) is MongoDB's
default authentication method. SCRAM requires a password for
each user.

The [authentication database](manual/core/security-users) for
SCRAM-authenticated users is the `admin` database.

> **Note:** By default, Atlas supports SCRAM-SHA-256 authentication.
> If you created a user before MongoDB 4.0, you must update
> MongoDB 4.0, update their passwords to generate SCRAM-SHA-256
> credentials. You may reuse existing passwords.

sections.heading: Authenticate Database Users
sections.body:
[X.509 Certificates](manual/core/security-x.509), also known
as mutual TLS or mTLS, allow passwordless authentication by using
a trusted certificate.

The [authentication database](manual/core/security-users) for
X.509-authenticated users is the `$external` database.

If you [LDAP authentication and authorization](ldaps-authentication-authorization), you can't connect to your
clusters with users that authenticate with an
Atlas-managed X.509 certificate. To enable LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) and
connecting to your clusters with X.509 users, see
[self-managed X.509 certificate](self-managed-x509).

sections.heading: Authenticate Database Users
sections.body:
You can create a database user which uses an [AWS IAM](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/introduction.html)
User or Role ARN (Amazon Resource Name) for authentication.

The [authentication database](manual/core/security-users) for
AWS IAM-authenticated users is the `$external` database.

AWS IAM authentication is available only on clusters which use MongoDB
version 7.0 and higher.

sections.heading: Add New Database Users
sections.body:
A project can have users with different authentication methods.

You cannot change a user's authentication method after creating that
user. To use an alternative authentication method, you must create a
new user.

Atlas CLI

To create a database user for your project using the Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas dbusers create [builtInRole]... [options]

```

To create a new Atlas-managed X.509 certificate for the specified database user using the Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas dbusers certs create [options]

```

To learn more about the syntax and parameters for the previous commands, see the Atlas CLI documentation for
[atlas dbusers create](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-create) and
[atlas dbusers certs create](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-certs-create).

Related Links

- [Install the Atlas CLI](atlas/cli/current/install-atlas-cli)

- [Connect to the Atlas CLI](atlas/cli/current/connect-atlas-cli)

Atlas Administration API

Atlas UI

Password Authentication

- If it isn't already displayed, click the
  Database Users tab.

- Click  Add New Database User.

In the Authentication Method section of the Add
New Database User modal window, select the box labeled Password.

Under Password Authentication, there are two text fields.

- Enter a username for the new user in the top text field.

- Enter a password for the new user in the lower text field.

To use a password auto-generated by Atlas,
click the Autogenerate Secure Password button.

Select the database user privileges. You can assign privileges to the new user
in one or more of the following ways:

- Select a [built-in role](manual/reference/built-in-roles) from the
  Built-in Role dropdown menu. You can select one
  built-in role per database user within the Atlas UI. If you delete the
  default option, you can click Add Built-in Role to select a new built-in role.

- If you have any [custom roles](mongodb-roles) defined, you can expand
  the Custom Roles section and select
  one or more roles from the Custom Roles dropdown menu. Click
  Add Custom Role to add more custom roles. You can also
  click the Custom Roles link to see the custom
  roles for your project.

- Expand the Specific Privileges section and select one or more
  [privileges](manual/reference/built-in-roles) from the
  Specific Privileges dropdown menu. Click
  Add Specific Privilege to add more privileges. This assigns the
  user specific privileges on individual databases and collections.

Atlas can apply a built-in role, multiple custom roles, and multiple specific
privileges to a single database user.

To remove an applied role or privilege, click 
Delete next to the role or privilege you wish to delete.

> **Note:** Atlas doesn't display the  Delete icon
> next to your Built-in Role, Custom Role, or
> Specific Privilege selection if you selected only one option. You
> can delete the selected role or privilege once you apply another role or privilege.

For more information on authorization, see [Role-Based
Access Control](manual/core/authorization) and [Built-in
Roles](manual/reference/built-in-roles) in the MongoDB manual.

By default, users can access all the clusters and federated database instances in the
project. You can restrict access to specific clusters and federated database instances
by doing the following:

- Toggle Restrict Access to Specific Clusters/Federated
  Database Instances to ON.

- Select the clusters and federated database instances to grant the user access to
  from the Grant Access To list.

Toggle Temporary User to On and choose
a time after which Atlas can delete the user from the
Temporary User Duration dropdown. You can select one of the
following time periods for the user to exist:

- 6 hours

- 1 day

- 1 week

In the Database Users tab, temporary users display
the time remaining until Atlas will delete the user. Once
Atlas deletes the user, any client or application that uses
the temporary user's credentials loses access to the cluster.

X.509 Certificates

- If it isn't already displayed, click the
  Database Users tab.

- Click  Add New Database User.

| Field           | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 |
| --------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Common Name     | The user's Common Name (CN) protected by the TLS/SSL certificate. For more information, see RFC 2253.For example, if your common name is "Jane Doe", your organization is "MongoDB", and your country is "US", insert the following into the Common Name field:CN=Jane Doe,O=MongoDB,C=US                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   |
| User Privileges | You can assign roles in one of the following ways:Select Atlas admin, which provides the user with readWriteAnyDatabase as well as a number of administrative privileges.Select readWriteAnyDatabase, which provides the user with privileges to read and write to any database.Select readAnyDatabase which provides the user with privileges to read any database.Select Select Custom Role to select a custom role previously created in Atlas. You can create custom roles for database users in cases where the built-in database user roles cannot describe the desired set of privileges. For more information on custom roles, see Configure Custom Database Roles.Click Add Default Privileges. When you click this option, you can select individual roles and specify the database on which the roles apply. Optionally, for the read and readWrite roles, you can also specify a collection. If you do not specify a collection for read and readWrite, the role applies to all non-system collections in the database.For more information on the built-in Atlas privileges, see Built-In Roles.For more information on authorization, see Role-Based Access Control and Built-in Roles in the MongoDB manual. |

AWS IAM

- If it isn't already displayed, click the
  Database Users tab.

- Click  Add New Database User.

In the Authentication Method section of the Add
New Database User modal window, select the box marked AWS IAM.

- Select a user type from the AWS IAM Type dropdown menu.

- Enter an AWS user ARN (Amazon Resource Name). Click the
  See instruction below link for help with finding your ARN.

Select the database user privileges. You can assign privileges to the new user
in one or more of the following ways:

- Select a [built-in role](manual/reference/built-in-roles) from the
  Built-in Role dropdown menu. You can select one
  built-in role per database user within the Atlas UI. If you delete the
  default option, you can click Add Built-in Role to select a new built-in role.

- If you have any [custom roles](mongodb-roles) defined, you can expand
  the Custom Roles section and select
  one or more roles from the Custom Roles dropdown menu. Click
  Add Custom Role to add more custom roles. You can also
  click the Custom Roles link to see the custom
  roles for your project.

- Expand the Specific Privileges section and select one or more
  [privileges](manual/reference/built-in-roles) from the
  Specific Privileges dropdown menu. Click
  Add Specific Privilege to add more privileges. This assigns the
  user specific privileges on individual databases and collections.

Atlas can apply a built-in role, multiple custom roles, and multiple specific
privileges to a single database user.

To remove an applied role or privilege, click 
Delete next to the role or privilege you wish to delete.

> **Note:** Atlas doesn't display the  Delete icon
> next to your Built-in Role, Custom Role, or
> Specific Privilege selection if you selected only one option. You
> can delete the selected role or privilege once you apply another role or privilege.

For more information on authorization, see [Role-Based
Access Control](manual/core/authorization) and [Built-in
Roles](manual/reference/built-in-roles) in the MongoDB manual.

By default, users can access all the clusters and federated database instances in the
project. You can restrict access to specific clusters and federated database instances
by doing the following:

- Toggle Restrict Access to Specific Clusters/Federated
  Database Instances to ON.

- Select the clusters and federated database instances to grant the user access to
  from the Grant Access To list.

Toggle Temporary User to On and choose
a time after which Atlas can delete the user from the
Temporary User Duration dropdown. You can select one of the
following time periods for the user to exist:

- 6 hours

- 1 day

- 1 week

In the Database Users tab, temporary users display
the time remaining until Atlas will delete the user. Once
Atlas deletes the user, any client or application that uses
the temporary user's credentials loses access to the cluster.

Connecting to Atlas using AWS IAM authentication with the
[mongosh](mongodb-shell) requires shell version v0.9.0 or higher.

Consider the following:

- Use your [AWS IAM credentials](https://console.aws.amazon.com/iam/home?region=us-west-2#/security_credentials),
  using your access key ID as your username and your secret key as your
  password.

- The `authSource` query parameter is `$external`, URL-encoded as
  `%24external`.

- The `authMechanism` query parameter is `MONGODB-AWS`.

  > **Example:** 
  >
  > ```sh
  > mongosh "mongodb+srv://<atlas-host-name>/test?authSource=%24external&authMechanism=MONGODB-AWS" --username <access-key-id> --password <secret-key>
  > ```

OIDC

Click  Add New Database User or Group.

> **Note:** Until you [Atlas Workforce IdP configuration](apply-oidc-idp), this button says 
> Add New Database User.

In the Authentication Method section, select Federated Auth.

> **Note:** Until you [enable Workload IdP for your organization](configure-oidc),
> you can't select this box.

a. In the Select Identity Provider section, select a configured
OIDC (OpenID Connect) Identity Provider.

- Specify either the user identifier or group identifier associated with
  your configured Workforce Identity Provider.

> **Note:** For Azure Entra ID users, this value maps to the Object Id of your
> Azure user group rather than user group name.

To assign privileges to the new user or group, do one or more of the
following tasks:

- Select a [built-in role](manual/reference/built-in-roles) from the
  Built-in Role dropdown menu.

  - You can select one built-in role per database group in the Atlas UI.

  - If you delete the default option, you can click Add Built-in Role
    to select a new built-in role.

- Select or add [custom roles](mongodb-roles).

  - If you have any custom roles defined, you can expand
    the Custom Roles section and select
    one or more roles from the Custom Roles dropdown menu.

  - Click Add Custom Role to add more custom roles.

  - Click the Custom Roles link to see the custom
    roles for your project.

- Add [privileges](manual/reference/built-in-roles).

  - Expand the Specific Privileges section and select one or more
    privileges from the Specific Privileges dropdown menu.

  - Click Add Specific Privilege to add more privileges. This assigns
    the group specific privileges on individual databases and collections.

- Remove an applied role or privilege.

  - Click 

     

    Delete

     next to the

    role or privilege to delete.

  > **Note:** Atlas doesn't display the  Delete icon
  > next to your Built-in Role, Custom Role, or
  > Specific Privilege selection if you selected only one option. You
  > can delete the selected role or privilege once you apply another role or privilege.

Atlas can apply a built-in role, multiple custom roles, and multiple specific
privileges to a database group.

To learn more about authorization, see [Role-Based
Access Control](manual/core/authorization) and [Built-in
Roles](manual/reference/built-in-roles) in the MongoDB manual.

By default, groups can access all the clusters and federated database instances in the
project. To restrict access to specific clusters and federated database instances:

- Toggle Restrict Access to Specific Clusters/Federated
  Database Instances to On.

- Select the clusters and federated database instances to grant the group access to
  from the Grant Access To list.

Toggle Temporary User or Temporary Group to
On and choose a time after which Atlas can delete the user
or group from the Temporary User Duration or
Temporary Group Duration dropdown. You can select one of the
following time periods for the group to exist:

- 6 hours

- 1 day

- 1 week

In the Database Users tab, temporary users or groups display
the time remaining until Atlas deletes the users or group. After
Atlas deletes the user or group, any client or application that uses
the temporary user's or group's credentials loses access to the cluster.

Do one of the following steps:

- If you added a user, click the Add User button.

- If you added a group, click the Add Group button.

LDAP

> **Note:** Starting with MongoDB 8.0, LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) authentication and authorization is
> deprecated. The feature is available and will continue to operate
> without changes throughout the lifetime of MongoDB 8. LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) will be
> removed in a future major release.
>
> For details, see [LDAP Deprecation](manual/core/LDAP-deprecation).

Follow the steps to [configure authorization with LDAP](config-auth-ldap), then follow the
steps to [Add an LDAP Database User or Group](add-db-user-group-ldap).

sections.heading: Add New Database Users
sections.body: The Atlas CLI uses the following commands to create new database users and X.509 certificates. The options you specify determine the authentication method.

sections.heading: Add New Database Users
sections.body:
You can add database users through the Atlas Administration API. The
options you specify determine the authentication method. To learn
more, see [Create One Database User](atlas/reference/api-resources-spec/v2).

sections.heading: Add New Database Users
sections.body:
Select an authentication mechanism and follow the steps to create
a new database user using the Atlas UI.

sections.heading: View Database Users and Certificates
sections.body: Atlas CLI

sections.heading: View Database Users
sections.body:
To list all Atlas database users for your project using the Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas dbusers list [options]

```

To return the details for a single Atlas database user in the project you specify using the Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas dbusers describe <username> [options]

```

To learn more about the syntax and parameters for the previous commands, see the Atlas CLI documentation for
[atlas dbusers list](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-list) and
[atlas dbusers describe](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-describe).

Related Links

- [Install the Atlas CLI](atlas/cli/current/install-atlas-cli)

- [Connect to the Atlas CLI](atlas/cli/current/connect-atlas-cli)

sections.heading: View X.509 Certificates for a Database User
sections.body:
To list all Atlas-managed, unexpired certificates for a database user using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas dbusers certs list <username> [options]

```

To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas dbusers certs list](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-certs-list).

Atlas Administration API

To view Atlas database users using the
Atlas Administration API, see [Get All](atlas/reference/api-resources-spec/v2).

Atlas UI

To view Atlas database users and X.509 certificates in the
Atlas UI:

sections.heading: Step 1: View  database users.
sections.body:
- If it's not already displayed, click the
  **Database Users** tab.

- Click pencil **Edit** for the user
  to view their privileges, authentication details, and
  X.509 certificates.

sections.heading: Modify Database Users
sections.body:
Atlas CLI

To update a database user from your project using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas dbusers update <username> [options]

```

To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas dbusers update](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-update).

Atlas Administration API

Atlas UI

sections.heading: Modify Database Users
sections.body:
You can update database users through the Atlas Administration API. To
learn more, see [Update One](atlas/reference/api-resources-spec/v2).

sections.heading: Modify Database Users
sections.body:
To modify existing users for an Atlas project:

- If it's not already displayed, click the
  Database Users tab.

- Click  Edit for the user you
  want to modify.

  You can modify the privileges and authentication details
  assigned to the user. You cannot modify the authentication
  method.

  The following table describes what you can do for each user:

  | User Type                             | Action                                                                                                                                |
  | ------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
  | SCRAM authenticated users             | Edit a user's password.                                                                                                               |
  | X.509 certificate authenticated users | Download a new certificate.                                                                                                           |
  | AWS IAM users                         | Modify database access privileges.                                                                                                    |
  | Temporary users                       | Modify the time period the user exists or make the user a permanent user, provided the user's expiration date has not already passed. |

  > **Note:** You cannot change a permanent user into a temporary user.
  > If you change a temporary user into a permanent user, you
  > cannot make it temporary again.

- Click Update User to save the changes.

sections.heading: Delete Database Users
sections.body:
Atlas CLI

To delete a database user from your project using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas dbusers delete <username> [options]

```

To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas dbusers delete](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-delete).

Atlas Administration API

Atlas UI

sections.heading: Delete Database Users
sections.body:
You can delete database users through the Atlas Administration API. To
learn more, see [Delete One](atlas/reference/api-resources-spec/v2).

sections.heading: Delete Database Users
sections.body:
To delete existing users for an Atlas project using the
Atlas UI:

sections.heading: Step 1: Delete an existing user.
sections.body:
- If it's not already displayed, click the
  **Database Users** tab.

- Click trash-o **Delete** next to
  the user you want to delete.

- Click **Delete** again to confirm.

- Click **Delete** again to confirm.
